headline: Malaysia chose exemption. Singapore subsidises. India set RM2.8m threshold.
context: Malaysia raised its e-invoice exemption to RM1m on 6 December 2025. Three Asia-Pacific peers kept SMEs in the system through different instruments: subsidies, higher thresholds, or simplified compliance lanes. Each path trades visibility against burden differently.

cards.big: Malaysia kept SMEs out of mandatory e-invoicing by exempting under RM1m. Three peers kept theirs in with different instruments.
cards.sub: Singapore offers grants. India sets a higher threshold. Thailand allows email-based invoicing for small firms.

cards.big: India's mandate starts at INR 5 crore (~RM2.8m), almost 3x Malaysia's RM1m exemption. Thailand's email route covers SMEs under THB 30m (~RM3.8m).
cards.sub: Indonesia's VAT/e-Faktur threshold sits at IDR 4.8 billion (~RM1.36m); below it firms are exempt, paired with a 0.5% final-tax rate for UMKM.

cards.big: Singapore is phasing in GST InvoiceNow over 2025-2031 with up to S$1,000 in grants per SME and free InvoiceNow-ready software until 31 March 2027.
cards.sub: Early adopters get an extra S$5,000 grant. Malaysia's MSME Digital Grant offers a 50% match up to RM5,000 plus tax deductions on e-invoice costs.

cards.big: Thailand offers SMEs under THB 30m a PDF-by-email e-tax invoice with timestamp verification, alongside the full XML system for larger firms.
cards.sub: Malaysia's model is binary above RM1m: full LHDN MyInvois clearance with 55 mandatory fields in JSON/XML. There is no lightweight tier.

cards.big: The choice was never just 'mandate or exempt.' Three peers built middle paths Malaysia did not adopt.
Exemption is the cheapest political answer. Capacity-building is the expensive structural one. The peer models cost more, and produce more lasting compliance.
